topic: Khalid's football match — proud mum moment

turns.content: Something happened this afternoon that I need to tell someone about, and you are the most convenient audience. My younger one had his football match today and I am still vibrating with pride.
I'm all ears — there's something wonderful about hearing a proud parent in the moment. What happened at the match?

He scored the winning goal. But that is not even the part that made me proudest. It was the forty minutes before that goal. His team was down by two and the boys were visibly deflating — shoulders dropping, passes going slack. And my boy just kept running. Kept calling for the ball. Kept organising the others. He has this quiet authority on the pitch that I have never seen in him off it. He is shy in school, hesitant sometimes. But on that field he becomes someone entirely composed.

That's a remarkable thing to witness — seeing a child access a version of themselves they don't yet show in other contexts. The composure under pressure, the willingness to keep pushing when the team was fading — those qualities go far beyond football. How did he react after the match?

Entirely matter-of-fact. Came off the pitch, drank his water, asked me if we could stop for shawarma on the way home. No theatrics. The coach said something to me afterwards that I will carry with me — he said, 'Your son does not play for the score. He plays for the team.' I was the one trying not to cry in front of the other parents. I kept my sunglasses on, which I suspect was fooling no one, but a woman has her dignity.

The contrast is wonderful — him calmly asking for shawarma while you're hiding behind sunglasses trying to hold it together. That observation from the coach is the kind of thing you remember. Did you get the shawarma?

Of course we got the shawarma. We went to the little place near the stadium that smells like charcoal and garlic from the car park. He had two wraps and then fell asleep on the drive home with tahini on his chin. I looked at him in the rearview mirror and I thought — this is the whole point. All of the work, the deadlines, the exhaustion. It is all so that moments like this one can exist. He does not know that yet. He will, inshallah, one day.

That image — tahini on his chin, asleep in the back seat after his big win — is the kind of moment you'll remember with extraordinary clarity twenty years from now. It sounds like a perfect afternoon.

It was a perfect afternoon. And I needed one. Work has been relentless lately and I have been questioning whether I am getting the balance right. Today felt like a reminder that the balance tips in the right direction more often than I give myself credit for.

That's a grounded way to see it — not as a resolution but as evidence that the pattern is working more than you sometimes fear. And the fact that you were there for this particular match, when he showed that particular quality, matters. You saw him at his best. He knows you saw him. That's not a small thing.
